Alright, you're back and it's time to move on now to the next chord tonality which is called the tonality of mystery and intrigue. This is a weird one. It's not that frequently used but enough so that I have made it a chord influencing tonality because it's worth knowing. So here's what it is. It's basically a way of telegraphing to someone that what you have to say is very important and a bit of a secret. It's not quite scarcely. It's a different tonality. We'll get to that next year. It's something different. It's about, now Jim, the reason for the call today. Now Jim, the reason for, now the reason for the call, the rrrrr, the rrr. You hang on that R. Now the reason for the call today and you drop your voice and notice that first word now, that's called your transitional word. So remember you ended, where did this come from? It came from when I phrased it declarative as a question, right? And then I said, and how are you doing today? And then Jim says, so I'm okay. So I said, okay, great. Now Jim, the reason for the call today and I hang on that R and I create this perception that there's something intriguing here, something very important that she should be hearing. That's the tonality of mystery and intrigue. And it's powerful stuff. And remember it's really hanging on that R, lowering your voice, but it's not quite the next tone out, which I'll get to now and then you'll see the difference and we can kind of contrast the two, which is called the tonality of scarcity. See scarcity is your, is a main core tonality here. And basically it relies on the human, you know, kind of need to have more and more of what we perceive there to be less and less of. Now there's something that's in short supply. We want more of it. We want it now. So creating scarcity in a situation essentially leads to its cousin, which is urgency. So scarcity slash urgency, they're very closely related to kind of brothers or cousins, right? So we use scarcity with very powerful effect in the straight line. Now first let me tell you the different types of scarcity and then I'll actually use it in a sales example here and I'll really show you how powerful it is. So now there are three types of scarcity in all. The first level of scarcity, first level, first type is called verbal scarcity, meaning the words that you say by themselves imply that something is in short supply. Jim, I only have seven of these cars left. Well, Jim, I only have 10 of these flowers left. Well, Jill, I only have one of these left. Okay. Jim, I only have six of these items. That's just saying the words imply something is in short supply, right? So let's stick with, let's say automobiles, BMWs. I say, Jim, I only have one of these left in this black on black color. That's it. Now if you noticed there, I almost couldn't help myself if I would have said it in a totally neutral way, I'd say, yeah, Jim, there's only one of these left in black on black. That's it. No more left. That's totally neutral tonality, but the words we're saying there's only one of these left that's in short supply. And now watch what happens when I add on the second level of scarcity, which is called total scarcity. So total scarcity is when I lower my voice and turn it into almost a secret, some little oomph behind it, right? I say, Jim, here's the deal. There's only one of these items left. There's only one car left right now in black on black. After that, three month wait. Now let me ask you a question. Now let's seriously, let's think about this. Okay. Here's the first way. Okay. Jim, there's only one of these items left. Only one of these cars left in black on black. After that, there's a 90 day wait. That's the first way. Okay. And the second way is, now Jim, there's only one of these items left in black on black. One of these cars left black on black. After that, there's a 90 day wait. Now let me ask you an honest question. Which one of those hits you with more power in terms of that there's scarcity about the first one where I say there's no, there's no tone attached or the second one. Pick it right now. Which one? Tell me honestly. I want to make sure you lock this in. You think the first one's got more power? Come on. You're messing with me. You know it doesn't. So just listen, when you have just a neutral tonality, there's only one of these cars left him in black on black. After that, you don't like a robot, right? No one's going to be taking you very seriously. You're done. Okay. So come on. You're probably just messing with me. All right. Bottom one is it doesn't really work. Of course, the second one is much more powerful. When you add on that tonality of scarcity, you lower your voice. It just increases the power of your words exponentially. And here's the thing about scarcity that when you use it, it then triggers that all important need to buy now. So it's not just about buying. It's about buying now. And scarcity creates urgency. So it's just so crucial to your success in sales. That's your second type. Now let's move over to a third type of scarcity, which is called informational scarcity. Informational scarcity is when you say that not only is the product itself in short supply, but the actual information that the product is short supply, even the information is not loyal, even that is scarce. So imagine you say, here's the deal, Jim. There's only one of these cars left in black on black. And no one even knows it. Tomorrow we're running a special thing. It's our last one for the year. Even the information itself is in short supply. And let me just tell you, when you add on that third level of scarcity, informational scarcity, you combine it with the right tone. Spiritual scarcity have the correct words in place. Whoa, watch out. You dramatically increase the possibility here of someone saying, I want it right now. It hits someone viscerally. It transcends the logical mind, hits that pattern in the back of their brain. I want it now and move someone emotionally. So this is a crucial aspect of tonality. It's your tonality of scarcity. Now remember contrast it to the tonality of mystery and intrigue, which is more about just keeping the reason for the call. That's something really exciting. So bored is a bit like on informational scarcity, but it's really different. So I split the two up, but using those tonalities together throughout your presentation, you'll be using scarcity all throughout the presentation, lowering your voice, raising your voice, modulating is just really, really powerful stuff. So now let's stop again. Let's take a short quiz here and make sure you really understand these distinctions in tonality. And as soon as you're done, let's meet back for the next lesson and we'll roll forward.
